teamObjective.explanation: Partner (player 2) is down to 2 cards, so the best immediate team goal is to lead a very low non-trump single that partner can capture and continue toward finishing their hand.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely holds cards they are trying to run out and may be able to beat a low diamond single; a low lead gives them the best chance to take control.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents have 3 cards each and will try to contest weak leads to stop partner from finishing, but they must spend a higher non-trump or a trump to do so.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of diamonds is the weakest non-trump single, maximizing the probability that partner can win the trick and keep playing toward an empty hand.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: A 6 is harder for a nearly-finished partner to beat and is still easily overtaken by opponents, so it transfers control less effectively.
Intermediate card; still raises the bar for partner to win and does not improve defense against opponents.
Better than 5 or 6 but still higher than necessary; a 3 gives partner the widest window to take the trick.

risks: Any opponent with a higher card or trump can win the trick and deny partner the lead.
If partner cannot beat even a 3, the lead passes to opponents who may then suppress the near-finished partner.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Play the absolute lowest legal card to minimize the strength partner needs to win, while keeping the 4, 5, and 6 for later tricks.

notes: No cards have been played yet, so beliefs are grounded only in the initial deal and public hand counts; partner intent is inferred probabilistically.
